name: Lancelot
description: Sir Lancelot, also known as Lancelot du Lac, is a prominent figure in Arthurian legend, often depicted as the greatest knight of the Round Table and a central character in the tragic love triangle involving King Arthur and Queen Guinevere. While primarily associated with the Arthurian cycle, which has roots in Celtic mythology and folklore, Lancelot himself is a later addition to the legendarium, likely originating in French romances of the 12th century. His character embodies the ideals of chivalry, courtly love, and martial prowess, yet he is also tragically flawed, ultimately contributing to the downfall of Camelot through his adulterous affair with the queen. Lancelot's story explores themes of loyalty, temptation, and the conflict between personal desires and societal obligations. His devotion to Guinevere, while romanticized, ultimately undermines his allegiance to Arthur and the stability of the kingdom, marking him as a complex and morally ambiguous figure. He is a symbol of both excellence and failure, a reminder that even the greatest heroes are susceptible to human frailties.
subtitle: The Greatest Knight

keyMyths.title: Lancelot and Guinevere
keyMyths.description: The illicit affair between Lancelot and Queen Guinevere is a central element of Arthurian legend. Their passionate love, while celebrated in some romantic interpretations, ultimately leads to the fracturing of the Round Table and the weakening of Arthur's kingdom. Lancelot's internal conflict between his love for Guinevere and his loyalty to Arthur highlights the destructive consequences of unchecked desire and the complexities of moral choices. The discovery of their affair triggers a series of events that culminate in civil war and the eventual downfall of Camelot.

keyMyths.title: Lancelot's Madness
keyMyths.description: Driven to madness by guilt and despair over his betrayal of Arthur and his separation from Guinevere, Lancelot wanders the wilderness. This period of mental anguish and self-imposed exile reflects the profound impact of his actions and the weight of his conscience. His madness serves as a symbolic representation of the inner turmoil and moral decay that plague the once-noble knight. He is eventually healed, but the experience leaves a lasting mark on his character, reinforcing his commitment to penance and atonement.
keyMyths.source: Chrétien de Troyes. *Lancelot, or the Knight of the Cart*.

keyMyths.title: Lancelot's Tournament Prowess
keyMyths.description: Lancelot's unparalleled skill in tournaments is a recurring motif in Arthurian literature. He consistently demonstrates his martial superiority, defeating numerous opponents and earning renown as the greatest knight of his time. His victories in tournaments are not merely displays of physical strength and combat proficiency; they also symbolize his embodiment of chivalric ideals and his dedication to upholding the principles of knighthood. However, his tournament victories are often tinged with a sense of hubris, foreshadowing his eventual downfall.

sources.author: Malory, Thomas
sources.description: A comprehensive compilation of Arthurian legends, providing a detailed account of Lancelot's life and deeds.
sources.text: A comprehensive compilation of Arthurian legends, providing a detailed account of Lancelot's life and deeds.

sources.author: Chrétien de Troyes
sources.work: Lancelot, or the Knight of the Cart

sources.author: Loomis, Roger Sherman
sources.work: Arthurian Tradition and Chrétien de Troyes
sources.description: A scholarly analysis of the development of Arthurian legend and the role of Chrétien de Troyes in shaping the character of Lancelot.
